claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period; Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
circumstances: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you find out if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

Let's start: **How many children are you looking to claim Child Benefit for, and what are their names and ages?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! I'm interested in claiming Child Benefit for my son Alex. He's 17 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information about Alex.

Since Alex is 17 years old, his eligibility for Child Benefit depends on his current situation. **Is Alex currently in approved education or training (such as A-levels, NVQs, or other qualifications up to and including A-level or equivalent)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not in any education or training at the moment. He finished school and decided not to continue with A-levels or anything like that.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that.

Since Alex is 17 and not in approved education or training, I need to ask one more question to fully assess his eligibility:

**Has Alex recently left education or training (within the last 20 weeks), or has he registered with a careers service, Connexions, or the Ministry of Defence as someone available for work or training?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he hasn't registered with any careers service or anything like that. He just left school and went straight into work.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Let me check the eligibility rules to give you an accurate assessment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided and the eligibility rules, I now have enough information to make an assessment for Alex.

Here's the situation:
- Alex is 17 years old
- He is not in approved education or training
- He has not registered with a careers service or similar
- He has gone straight into work

According to the rules, Child Benefit normally continues for children aged 16-19 only if they stay in **approved education or training**. Additionally, you can apply for Child Benefit to continue for 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education and **registers with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services** - but Alex hasn't done this.

Furthermore, the rules state that Child Benefit **stops** if your child "starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training."

Let me confirm one detail: **Is Alex working 24 hours or more per week?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he's working full-time at a local shop. He's there most days of the week.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for confirming that. I now have all the information I need to make an assessment for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, I'm sorry to say that **you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

Here's why:

1. **Age requirement**: For children aged 16 or over, Child Benefit normally only continues if they stay in approved education or training. Alex is 17 and not in any education or training.

2. **Careers service registration**: A 16 or 17 year old who leaves education can still qualify for Child Benefit for up to 20 weeks if they register with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services. Alex hasn't done this.

3. **Full-time work**: Crucially, the rules state that Child Benefit **stops** when a child starts working 24 hours or more per week while not in approved education or training. Since Alex is working full-time at the local shop, this rule applies.

If Alex's circumstances change in the future—for example, if he decides to return to approved education or training—you may become eligible again at that point.

I hope this helps clarify the situation. If you have any other questions, please feel free to ask.

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and is not eligible for Child Benefit for the following reasons: 1) Child Benefit normally continues for children aged 16-19 only if they remain in approved education or training - Alex is not in any education or training. 2) A 16 or 17 year old who leaves education can qualify for Child Benefit for 20 weeks if they register with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services - Alex has not done this. 3) Most importantly, the rules state that Child Benefit stops if the child 'starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training' - Alex is working full-time (more than 24 hours per week) at a local shop and is not in approved education or training. Therefore, Alex does not meet the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is inquiring about Child Benefit for their 17-year-old son Alex, who has left school, is not in any approved education or training, has not registered with a careers service, and is now working full-time. Under Child Benefit rules, eligibility for children aged 16 and over requires them to be in approved education or training, or to have registered with a careers service within 20 weeks of leaving education. Additionally, Child Benefit explicitly stops when a child starts working 24 or more hours per week while not in approved education or training. As Alex is working full-time and not in education, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for him.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 17 years old and is not eligible for Child Benefit for the following reasons: 1) Child Benefit normally continues for children aged 16-19 only if they remain in approved education or training - Alex is not in any education or training. 2) A 16 or 17 year old who leaves education can qualify for Child Benefit for 20 weeks if they register with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services - Alex has not done this. 3) Most importantly, the rules state that Child Benefit stops if the child 'starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training' - Alex is working full-time (more than 24 hours per week) at a local shop and is not in approved education or training. Therefore, Alex does not meet the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is inquiring about Child Benefit for their 17-year-old son Alex, who has left school, is not in any approved education or training, has not registered with a careers service, and is now working full-time. Under Child Benefit rules, eligibility for children aged 16 and over requires them to be in approved education or training, or to have registered with a careers service within 20 weeks of leaving education. Additionally, Child Benefit explicitly stops when a child starts working 24 or more hours per week while not in approved education or training. As Alex is working full-time and not in education, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for him.
